stem: A 6-year-old girl is referred by her GP for breast development first noticed by her mother 8 months ago. Her mother also reports the recent appearance of fine axillary hair, mild adult-type body odour, and accelerated linear growth (she has crossed two centile lines on the height chart in the past year, from 50th to 90th centile). She has had no headaches, no visual symptoms, no seizures, no abdominal pain and no vaginal bleeding. There is no neonatal history of seizures or hypothalamic insult. There is no exposure to topical oestrogen, no parental medications she could be accessing, no oestrogen-containing supplements in the household, and no soy-heavy diet. Her past medical and developmental history is unremarkable and there is no family history of central or peripheral precocious puberty, McCune-Albright syndrome, neurofibromatosis or congenital adrenal hyperplasia. On examination she has Tanner stage 3 breast development with palpable glandular tissue, Tanner stage 2 pubic hair, fine axillary hair on inspection of the underarm, no acne, no cafe-au-lait macules, no neurofibromas and no clitoromegaly. The thyroid is not palpable, the abdomen is soft with no palpable masses and no organomegaly, and the neurological examination including fundoscopy and visual fields by confrontation is normal. Bone age performed today is 9 years. Pelvic ultrasound shows a uterine length of 4.2 cm with an endometrial echo and bilateral ovaries with multiple small follicles and no ovarian cyst or mass.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation to distinguish central from peripheral precocious puberty?

options.text: Pelvic MRI to look for an ovarian sex cord-stromal tumour producing oestradiol
options.rationale: The pelvic ultrasound has already shown a pubertal pattern without an ovarian mass; pelvic MRI is reserved for ultrasound abnormality or suspected peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

options.text: GnRH (or analogue) stimulation test measuring LH and FSH at 0, 30 and 60 minutes
options.rationale: GnRH (or GnRH analogue) stimulation testing with peak LH >=5 IU/L (or LH:FSH ratio >0.66) confirms central precocious puberty, while a suppressed LH response with elevated oestradiol points to a peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty and ESPE consensus.

options.text: Serum 17-OH progesterone and DHEAS to exclude non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia
options.rationale: Non-classical CAH causes premature adrenarche (pubic and axillary hair, body odour) without breast development; this child has Tanner B3 breast development, indicating gonadarche per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Empirical commencement of a GnRH analogue (leuprolide) and monitor for breast regression
options.rationale: Treatment should not precede confirmation of the diagnosis and underlying cause (including MRI brain for central PP in a girl under 6-8 years) per APEG position statement.

options.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol to look for a hypothalamic hamartoma before any biochemical testing
options.rationale: MRI brain is performed once central precocious puberty is biochemically confirmed (or in girls under 6 with central features) but is not the first discriminating test between central and peripheral per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric endocrinology outpatient clinic by her GP with a 3-month history of progressive unintentional weight loss of 6 kg despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, fine tremor, palpitations, mild anxiety, oligomenorrhoea and poor sleep, and a recent decline in academic performance noted by her teachers. Her mother has noticed that her eyes appear more prominent and that she has a fine tremor of the outstretched hands and a tendency to fidget. There is no recent viral illness, no anterior neck pain, no exogenous thyroid hormone or iodine-containing medication exposure, and no family history of thyroid disease, although a maternal cousin has type 1 diabetes mellitus and her grandmother has vitiligo. On examination she has a smooth diffuse non-tender goitre approximately twice normal size with an audible bruit on auscultation, mild bilateral proptosis with lid lag and lid retraction, fine resting tremor, warm sweaty palms, and a resting heart rate of 124 bpm in sinus rhythm. There is no pretibial myxoedema and no thyroid acropachy. Her bloods show TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L (reference 9-19), free T3 18 pmol/L (reference 3.5-6.5), anti-TSH receptor antibody 12 IU/L (reference <1.75), and a normal full blood count including a neutrophil count of 4.2 x 10^9/L with normal LFTs.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line medical management?

options.text: Propylthiouracil 5-10 mg/kg/day orally as preferred thionamide due to peripheral T4-to-T3 block
options.rationale: PTU is contraindicated as first-line in paediatric Graves' due to a black-box warning for severe hepatotoxicity and acute liver failure; carbimazole is first-line per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Definitive radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation as initial therapy without a trial of thionamide
options.rationale: Initial therapy is medical with thionamide for 18-24 months; radioiodine is offered after relapse or in older adolescents with persistent disease per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism.

options.text: Total thyroidectomy under general anaesthesia as first-line definitive therapy now
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for thionamide failure or intolerance, very large goitre, or Graves' ophthalmopathy where radioiodine is unsuitable per ESPE consensus.

options.text: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day orally in divided doses plus propranolol 1-2 mg/kg/day, monitor FBC/LFT
options.rationale: Australian and ESPE-recommended first-line therapy for paediatric Graves' disease is a thionamide (carbimazole preferred to propylthiouracil because of PTU hepatotoxicity in children) at 0.5-1 mg/kg/day with a beta-blocker for adrenergic symptoms, baseline and serial neutrophil and LFT monitoring per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism and Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

options.text: Lugol's iodine 5-10 drops three times daily as monotherapy until normalisation of free T4
options.rationale: Inorganic iodine is used pre-operatively or in thyroid storm but not as monotherapy for chronic Graves' management per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred from his GP for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia of 4 months' duration. His parents estimate his fluid intake at approximately 4 litres per day, including overnight water bottles, and he is waking 3-4 times nightly to drink and urinate, with secondary nocturnal enuresis after being dry at night for the previous 18 months. He has had a 1 kg weight loss but is otherwise growing on the 25th centile and his pubertal stage is Tanner 1 as expected. There is no history of head injury, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no anosmia and no symptoms of diabetes mellitus such as polyphagia. His past history includes Langerhans cell histiocytosis diagnosed 18 months ago at age 3 with cutaneous and bony involvement, treated with vinblastine and prednisolone, that has been in apparent remission on surveillance imaging. On examination he is alert, well perfused, with normal vital signs and an unremarkable systemic examination, including normal fundoscopy and visual field examination by confrontation. Bloods today show Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, urea 6.2 mmol/L, creatinine 38 micromol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, calcium 2.4 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 305 mOsm/kg with a paired urine osmolality of 110 mOsm/kg measured on a morning sample. His urinary specific gravity is 1.002 and the urine dipstick is otherwise negative.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Formal water deprivation test with paired plasma and urine osmolality, then desmopressin challenge
options.rationale: A water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in the face of hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma; the diagnosis is already biochemically established and a desmopressin trial can proceed directly per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus and ESPE consensus.

options.text: Desmopressin trial with measurement of urine osmolality response, followed by MRI brain with pituitary protocol
options.rationale: When plasma is already hyperosmolar (>295 mOsm/kg) with hypernatraemia and inappropriately dilute urine (<300 mOsm/kg), DI is biochemically confirmed and a desmopressin trial (urine concentrates >50% = central DI; no response = nephrogenic DI) with MRI brain (looking for loss of posterior pituitary bright spot, infiltrative disease) is appropriate, particularly with this child's LCH history per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus.

options.text: Renal ultrasound and urinary tract DMSA scan to assess for medullary cystic disease
options.rationale: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no response to desmopressin, but is not the first-line discriminator per BMJ Best Practice: Diabetes Insipidus.

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test to exclude type 1 diabetes mellitus as the cause
options.rationale: Random glucose of 4.8 mmol/L and a urine specific gravity of 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis from glucose; this is hypotonic polyuria not diabetes mellitus per RCH CPG.

options.text: 24-hour urinary calcium and serum parathyroid hormone to exclude hypercalciuric nephrogenic DI
options.rationale: Calcium is normal at 2.4 mmol/L and chronic hypercalcaemia is an unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is the most likely diagnosis given the history per ESPE consensus.

stem: A 9-year-old previously well girl presents to the tertiary paediatric ED with 3 days of fever, headache and confusion, and one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes at home that resolved spontaneously without intervention. Her mother describes intermittent inappropriate laughter and word-finding difficulty for the last 24 hours and a sense that the child has been smelling something burnt that nobody else in the household can smell. There is no rash and no neck stiffness, and she has had no foreign travel, no mosquito-exposed regional travel to northern Australia or to Murray-Darling river settings, and no animal bites or scratches. Her immunisations are up to date for the standard NIP schedule (no Japanese encephalitis vaccine because of no risk exposure recorded). She is taking no regular medications and has no known allergies. On examination she has a low-grade fever, her GCS fluctuates between 13 and 15, she has subtle right-sided pronator drift and expressive dysphasia, and the rest of the cranial nerve and peripheral neurological examinations are normal. There are no purpuric lesions on full skin survey. CT brain is unremarkable. MRI is scheduled for the morning. LP is performed: opening pressure 22 cm H2O, CSF clear, WCC 96 x 10^6/L (90% lymphocytes), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (paired serum 5.1), Gram stain negative; CSF HSV, VZV, enterovirus and meningococcal PCR are pending, and an autoimmune encephalitis panel including anti-NMDAR antibodies has been sent.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management decision?

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (~20 mg/kg) q8h empirically while awaiting CSF HSV PCR
options.rationale: Olfactory hallucinations, dysphasia, temporal-lobe-localising features and a lymphocytic CSF in a child are HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise. Empirical IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (about 20 mg/kg under 12 years) q8h must be started immediately because delay increases mortality and morbidity, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for presumed anti-NMDAR encephalitis pending antibody results today
options.rationale: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a differential but immunotherapy without first excluding and treating HSV is dangerous because steroids worsen untreated HSV. Empirical aciclovir is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg and dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, withhold antivirals until PCR results
options.rationale: The CSF is lymphocytic with normal glucose, making bacterial meningitis very unlikely; HSV must be empirically covered with aciclovir without waiting for PCR results, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Arrange urgent EEG and defer all therapy until non-convulsive status epilepticus is fully excluded
options.rationale: EEG is useful for diagnosis (temporal lobe periodic discharges in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR) but is not a reason to delay empirical aciclovir in suspected encephalitis, per RCH CPG.

options.text: Commence IV ribavirin for suspected Japanese encephalitis given the recent Murray Valley exposure risk
options.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for Japanese encephalitis and no Murray Valley exposure is described. JEV is supportive care only, with vaccination as prevention, per NCIRS Japanese encephalitis vaccine.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP after his father, a recently arrived migrant from a high-tuberculosis-incidence country in South-East Asia, was diagnosed last week with smear-positive cavitating pulmonary tuberculosis sensitive to all first-line drugs on rapid molecular testing. The boy has been well, has no cough, no fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss and has gained weight along the 50th centile on his growth chart. He has not received BCG vaccination as it is not on the routine Australian schedule. He shares a bedroom with his father and has slept in the same bed during the 8 weeks prior to his father's diagnosis. He has no other identifiable contacts of concern, including childcare contacts, who have been screened by the public health unit. On examination he is well, alert and interactive, with no peripheral or cervical lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and a clear chest on auscultation. There is no erythema nodosum and no phlyctenular conjunctivitis. The local public health unit asks the GP to coordinate his contact assessment. A chest X-ray is performed and is reported as normal. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test using 0.1 mL of 10 TU/mL purified protein derivative is placed in the left forearm and read by the public health nurse at 72 hours, showing an induration of 12 mm with surrounding erythema. HIV serology is non-reactive. The mother asks whether an interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) such as QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus would be more accurate in her son and whether he needs treatment.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the Mantoux in 8 weeks because 12 mm without BCG may be non-specific
options.rationale: An induration of 12 mm in a non-BCG vaccinated child with a household smear-positive contact is well above the 10 mm threshold and is treated as latent TB infection without repeating the test, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Reassure that the Mantoux is negative because it is under 15 mm and discharge with no further investigation
options.rationale: In Australia, a Mantoux of 10 mm or more in a non-BCG vaccinated person, or 5 mm or more in HIV or other significant immunosuppression, is considered positive, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Order IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) instead because it is more specific and avoid further skin testing
options.rationale: IGRA has reduced sensitivity in children under 5 years and is not preferred as a sole test in this age group in Australian guidance; Mantoux remains the test of choice for young children, although IGRA can supplement in BCG-vaccinated children, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

options.text: Start a 6-month course of rifampicin monotherapy at 10 mg/kg daily without isoniazid today
options.rationale: Rifampicin monotherapy 4 months is an alternative regimen but the Australian default for paediatric latent TB infection is isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) for 6 to 9 months with pyridoxine, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CDNA SoNG.

options.text: Diagnose latent TB and commence isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine
options.rationale: A non-BCG child with a household smear-positive contact and Mantoux 10 mm or more, normal CXR and no clinical features of active disease has latent TB infection. The standard Australian paediatric regimen is isoniazid 10 mg/kg (max 300 mg) daily for 9 months with pyridoxine to prevent peripheral neuropathy; isoniazid plus rifampicin for 3 months is an alternative, as per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

stem: An 8-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, currently in the consolidation phase of a Berlin-Frankfurt-Munster protocol and on dexamethasone, vincristine and intrathecal methotrexate, is brought to the day oncology unit after a confirmed face-to-face household exposure to his 5-year-old sister, who developed a vesicular varicella rash on her trunk and face this morning. He has not had clinical varicella infection at any time in the past, did not complete the varicella vaccination schedule because of his oncology diagnosis at the time of his routine 18-month appointment, and was last on the day oncology ward yesterday for his scheduled intrathecal chemotherapy. His mother is also concerned about her own immunity because she is 18 weeks pregnant with the family's fourth child, has no history of chickenpox or vaccination herself, and has just had a serology test sent that morning to clarify her immune status. There is also a third sibling, a 12-year-old who has had clinically diagnosed varicella as a preschooler. The patient is currently afebrile and entirely asymptomatic, with no rash, no oral lesions and no respiratory or constitutional symptoms; his most recent full blood count 2 days ago showed a WCC of 1.8 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.7 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.9 x 10^9/L, and platelets 84 x 10^9/L. His baseline varicella IgG taken at diagnosis was negative. The exposure occurred 36 hours ago, and the local paediatric oncology consultant is available by phone.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate intervention for this patient?

options.text: Live attenuated varicella vaccine 0.5 mL subcutaneously within 5 days of exposure
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live vaccine and is contraindicated in significantly immunocompromised patients such as those on chemotherapy, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and Therapeutic Guidelines.

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 20 mg/kg q8h for 7 days starting from day 7 after exposure
options.rationale: Prophylactic oral aciclovir or valaciclovir from day 7 post-exposure is an alternative when VZIG is unavailable, but VZIG (or IVIG if VZIG unavailable) is preferred in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Antivirals also do not replace VZIG in this group when VZIG is available, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly for 5 days starting immediately today
options.rationale: Immediate aciclovir is treatment of established varicella, not pre-symptomatic prophylaxis; VZIG is the preferred PEP in significantly immunocompromised contacts, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Observation only and IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h if a rash develops
options.rationale: Observation alone misses the window for passive immunisation. VZIG within 96 hours (up to 10 days per current product information) substantially attenuates disease in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Treatment with IV aciclovir is correct only if disease develops, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

options.text: Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure (up to 10 days)
options.rationale: Significantly immunocompromised varicella-susceptible contacts (including children on chemotherapy, transplant recipients, and those on high-dose corticosteroids) should receive VZIG as soon as possible after exposure, ideally within 96 hours but up to 10 days. Other indicated groups include non-immune pregnant women and neonates of mothers with peripartum varicella (5 days before to 2 days after delivery), as per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and the Australian Immunisation Handbook.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by her parents on day 10 of consolidation chemotherapy with high-dose methotrexate and cytarabine. She has a tunnelled Hickman line in situ for 4 months, has been febrile for 4 hours with a single reading of 38.6 deg C documented at home by her parents, has had new rigors over the last 30 minutes, and her parents report increasingly rapid breathing and that she has been progressively more lethargic over the last hour with reduced eye contact. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is mottled with cold peripheries and a capillary refill of 5 seconds, BP 78/40 mmHg, HR 162 bpm, RR 40 /min, SpO2 94% on 4 L/min nasal prongs, T 39.4 deg C. There is no obvious focus of infection on rapid head-to-toe examination, the Hickman exit site is clean and not erythematous, her chest is clear, and there is no abdominal tenderness or perianal lesion. Her full blood count two days ago showed neutrophils 0.2 x 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.4 x 10^9/L, haemoglobin 92 g/L, and platelets 28 x 10^9/L. She has no penicillin or other drug allergy, no previous resistant organisms colonising her central line or stool surveillance, and her last echocardiogram performed 3 months ago for routine anthracycline monitoring showed normal LV function and a normal ejection fraction of 64%. There is no history of mucositis bleeding, no recent abdominal pain and no diarrhoea. A repeat FBC, blood cultures drawn from both Hickman lumens and a peripheral site simultaneously, venous lactate, VBG, EUC, LFTs, coagulation, group and screen, urinalysis and a portable CXR are being arranged in parallel with resuscitation. The on-call oncology consultant and the paediatric intensive care registrar have been contacted and are aware.
lead_in: After 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus is initiated, what is the single most appropriate empirical antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: IV cefepime 50 mg/kg q8h as monotherapy for febrile neutropenia, omitting empirical aminoglycoside
options.rationale: Cefepime monotherapy is acceptable for stable febrile neutropenia per some guidelines but the patient is in septic shock with hypotension and prolonged neutropenia. In Australian paediatric practice, piperacillin-tazobactam plus an aminoglycoside (or vancomycin if line infection suspected) is preferred for haemodynamically unstable febrile neutropenia, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily plus IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is a critical pathogen in febrile neutropenia. Antipseudomonal cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus IV gentamicin, add vancomycin if line infection suspected or shock persists
options.rationale: High-risk febrile neutropenia with septic shock and an indwelling Hickman line in a profoundly neutropenic patient requires antipseudomonal beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside for synergy and Gram-negative cover. Piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg (piperacillin component) q6h plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily is standard, with vancomycin added if suspected central line infection, mucositis, MRSA risk, or ongoing instability, as per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: IV meropenem 20 mg/kg eight-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy without empirical aminoglycoside
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line use when there is known ESBL colonisation, severe sepsis not responding to first-line therapy, or significant beta-lactam allergy; routine first-line use risks selecting carbapenem-resistant organisms, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Delay antibiotics until blood cultures return positive to ensure organism-directed therapy today
options.rationale: Antibiotics must be administered within 60 minutes (ideally within 30 minutes for septic shock) in suspected febrile neutropenia. Each hour of delay increases mortality, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

stem: A 38-hour-old term male born at 39+5 by NVD to a G2P1 mother with an uneventful pregnancy is brought to the special care nursery by the midwife. He has had three episodes of vomiting in the last 90 minutes, the most recent two clearly bilious (bright green) and photographed on the midwife's phone for the registrar to review. He passed meconium at 18 hours of age and has been breastfeeding poorly since the second feed, latching but suckling weakly and falling asleep. Birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 3.25 kg, representing a 4% drop. On examination he is irritable with HR 178, RR 62, CRT 3 seconds, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% in room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen without obvious mass, soft, with sluggish bowel sounds. There is no inguinal hernia, no anal stenosis, and no perianal abnormality. First-degree relatives have no history of cystic fibrosis or congenital gut malformation. Antenatal scans had not commented on intra-abdominal anatomy beyond reporting a normal anatomy scan at 20 weeks, with no polyhydramnios noted. An NG tube is placed and immediately drains 25 mL of bilious fluid with relief of distension. The neonatal registrar has IV access through a peripheral cannula and is preparing maintenance fluids and a 10 mL/kg saline bolus.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Upper GI contrast study within the next hour, paediatric surgical team aware and theatre standby
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation, showing an abnormal duodeno-jejunal junction position and a corkscrew or beak appearance if volvulus is present. This must occur with surgical standby because volvulus requires laparotomy within minutes to hours to prevent loss of the midgut (RCH CPG; ANZNN).

options.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids overnight, with reassessment in the morning
options.rationale: Watch-and-wait management of bilious vomiting in a neonate can result in complete loss of the midgut within 4-6 hours of established volvulus. This is a surgical emergency (RCH CPG).

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound looking for the whirlpool sign before considering further imaging today
options.rationale: USS whirlpool sign (SMA-SMV inversion with twisted mesentery) is supportive but not gold-standard, and absence of the sign does not exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast remains the definitive investigation (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph alone to exclude duodenal atresia as the diagnosis
options.rationale: Plain AXR is useful (may show double-bubble for duodenal atresia, or paucity of distal gas in volvulus) but is insufficient to confirm or exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast is required (RCH CPG).

options.text: Contrast enema looking for microcolon to exclude meconium ileus as diagnosis
options.rationale: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant who passed meconium at 18 hours it is less likely than malrotation. Upper GI contrast is the priority investigation for bilious vomiting (RCH CPG).

stem: A 28+5 week preterm female born at birth weight 1.05 kg is now on day 11 of life in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and trophic feeds of expressed breast milk were introduced on day 5 and advanced to 60 mL/kg/day by day 10. Over the last 12 hours she has had two large bilious gastric aspirates, abdominal distension increasing from 25 cm to 28.5 cm girth, and one episode of bright red blood in the stool. She has become apnoeic with three desaturations to less than 80% in the last hour requiring stimulation. HR has risen from 145 to 178. T is 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 mmHg with mean arterial pressure 30. CRT is 4 seconds. The abdominal wall is shiny with bluish discolouration over the right flank. WCC has fallen from 9.2 to 4.1 x10^9/L, platelets from 224 to 86, CRP risen from less than 5 to 92 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L. The supine abdominal radiograph shows multiple dilated loops of bowel with intramural gas (pneumatosis intestinalis) and a curvilinear lucency over the liver consistent with portal venous gas. There is no free intraperitoneal gas on a left lateral decubitus film. The paediatric surgical service has been consulted by phone.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood culture results are available to avoid promoting antibiotic resistance and unnecessary coverage
options.rationale: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must be commenced immediately on suspicion of NEC. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy (RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at current volume and add oral probiotics to reduce bacterial gut translocation
options.rationale: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM. Continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury and risks perforation. Probiotics are used prophylactically in some units but have no role in established NEC (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparotomy regardless of radiographic findings of pneumatosis or perforation
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, clinical deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is managed medically with surgical standby (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Nil by mouth, OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, TPN, serial films, paediatric surgical involvement
options.rationale: This is Bell stage IIB NEC (pneumatosis with portal venous gas, no perforation). Management is medical: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (penicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines), TPN, serial AXR every 6 hours for 48-72 hours to detect perforation. Surgery is indicated for perforation (Bell IIIB) or clinical deterioration (RCH CPG NEC; ANZNN).

options.text: Place a peritoneal drain at the bedside as definitive treatment without commencing antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable extremely-low-birth-weight infants with perforation, not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. There is no demonstrated perforation here (ANZNN).

stem: A 22-month-old girl is referred by her maternal and child health nurse for faltering growth. Her birth weight was on the 75th centile after an uncomplicated term pregnancy and NVD. She was exclusively breastfed until 6 months when solids were introduced including wheat-based cereals, pasta and bread, with normal progression of feeding milestones over the next several months. She is now on the 9th centile for weight and 25th for height, having crossed two major weight centiles downward over the last 8 months. Her mother reports chronic loose, bulky, pale, foul-smelling stools 3 to 5 times daily that float in the toilet, marked abdominal distension that develops over the course of the day, irritability particularly in the late afternoon, and a recent reduction in appetite. She has had no fevers, no overseas travel, no antibiotic exposure in the last 12 months, and is up to date with all routine immunisations. The paternal grandmother has type 1 diabetes mellitus and an older sibling aged 7 has confirmed coeliac disease diagnosed two years ago on duodenal biopsy. On examination today she is pale, thin, with mild abdominal distension, no organomegaly, no clubbing, and reduced subcutaneous fat over the buttocks and proximal thighs. The GP orders anti-tTG IgA and total IgA as the initial screen. Anti-tTG IgA returns at 230 U/mL (reference less than 20, more than 10 times upper limit), total IgA is 1.6 g/L (normal for age, excluding IgA deficiency), endomysial antibody is positive, and HLA-DQ2 is positive. The mother attends to discuss the results and asks the GP what should happen next. She is keen to start a strict gluten-free diet immediately because she has already done so for the older sibling and knows the supermarket products.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue gluten, refer paediatric gastroenterology for duodenal biopsy, only start gluten-free after histology; baseline nutritional studies; screen relatives
options.rationale: Australian and ESPGHAN guidelines require duodenal biopsy in symptomatic children with positive serology before commencing a gluten-free diet, unless the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway is fully met (anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit of normal AND positive EMA on a separate sample AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive) and this is delivered in a specialist paediatric gastroenterology setting with full counselling. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can invalidate the histology if the family later wants definitive diagnosis. First-degree relative screening is recommended (RACGP; Coeliac Australia; ESPGHAN).

options.text: Commence a strict gluten-free diet immediately given the strongly positive serology and family history, with no further investigation needed
options.rationale: Initiating a gluten-free diet before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway delivered by paediatric gastroenterology with full criteria met on two separate serology samples. Starting too early can render later confirmatory biopsy non-diagnostic if uncertainty arises (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Discontinue gluten for 4 weeks then perform a gluten challenge with repeat serology and biopsy
options.rationale: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes the diagnostic biopsy unreliable. Gluten challenge is reserved for cases where the original diagnosis was made without adequate evidence or there is later diagnostic doubt, and is usually deferred until age 5 or later because of impact on growth (ESPGHAN).

options.text: Repeat anti-tTG IgA in 6 months and only proceed to further investigation if titres remain elevated
options.rationale: A symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA, and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed by 6 months of repeat serology. Definitive diagnosis enables treatment that drives catch-up growth (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Order an IgG-based gliadin antibody panel and faecal fat studies as the next investigations
options.rationale: IgG anti-gliadin testing has been superseded by anti-tTG and EMA. Faecal fat is rarely used in modern paediatric coeliac diagnosis. Duodenal biopsy is the definitive next step (RACGP; ESPGHAN).
